runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) - empirical work on EITC labor supply responses
Keane & Moffitt (1998) - meta-analysis of labor supply elasticities across income groups
Chetty et al. (2013) - bounds on behavioral responses in tax design
runs.reasoningSummary: Lower-income primary earners (decile 1) typically show modest but meaningful labor supply responses to tax-benefit incentives. Empirical literature suggests substitution elasticities in this group are lower than upper-income workers, often in the 0.1–0.5 range. The lower bound reflects uncertainty about whether behavioral responses materialize at all for the poorest decile, while the upper tail acknowledges evidence of meaningful responses, particularly for those with EITC eligibility. Point estimate of 0.25 represents a moderate response typical of low-income primary earner studies.

(2013) - bounds on behavioral responses in tax design"], "reasoning_summary": "Lower-income primary earners (decile 1) typically show modest but meaningful labor supply responses to tax-benefit incentives. Empirical literature suggests substitution elasticities in this group are lower than upper-income workers, often in the 0.1\u20130.5 range. The lower bound reflects uncertainty about whether behavioral responses materialize at all for the poorest decile, while the upper tail acknowledges evidence of meaningful responses, particularly for those with EITC eligibility. Point estimate of 0.25 represents a moderate response typical of low-income primary earner studies."}

runs.reasoningSummary:

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the lowest earnings decile (decile 1) typically face higher marginal tax rates due to phase-out of transfer programs (EITC, TANF, etc.), creating strong work incentives for small income changes. Literature suggests substitution elasticities for lower-income groups are modest but non-trivial, typically in the range of 0.1–0.5 in elasticity terms. The first decile likely exhibits somewhat lower behavioral response than higher deciles due to adjustment costs and constraints, but positive response to effective wage changes is expected. Uncertainty is substantial due to program complexity and heterogeneity in the population.
